phase1.output:
a + b = 13
a - b = 7
a * b = 30
a / b = 3
a % b = 1
a > b? true
a == b? false
x && y = false
x || y = true
!x = false
Max: 10
phase1.explanation:
Java has several types of operators:

Arithmetic: + - * / %
• / does integer division (10/3 = 3, not 3.333)
• % gives remainder (10%3 = 1)

Relational: > < >= <= == !=
• Compare values, returns boolean

Logical: && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT)
• && — both must be true
• || — at least one must be true
• ! — flips the value

Ternary: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse
• Shorthand for if-else
• (a > b) ? a : b means "if a > b, use a, otherwise use b"

Pro tip: Use parentheses to make operator order clear!

phase2.errorMessage:
OperatorsExample.java:4: error: bad operand types for binary operator '+'
        System.out.println("a / b = " + a / b);
                                      ^
  first type:  String
  second type: int
phase2.explanation:
Java evaluates left to right with operator precedence!

"a / b = " + a / b is evaluated as ("a / b = " + a) / b — you can't divide a string by an int!

The fix: use parentheses to force the division first:
"a / b = " + (a / b)

Always wrap arithmetic in parentheses when combining with strings!

phase3.prompt:
Write a program that:
1. Creates variables x=15, y=4
2. Prints: x + y = 19, x - y = 11, x * y = 60, x / y = 3, x % y = 3
3. Uses ternary to print which is bigger: "15 is bigger" or "4 is bigger"
dialogueHints: Use the ternary operator: condition ? value1 : value2
Use String concatenation to build your output messages.
phase3.openingDialogue: Time to crunch some numbers! Show me what you've got~ 🧮
